items.label: Evidence A
items.name: journalist_intake_notes.txt
items.content:
JOURNALIST INTAKE NOTES
=======================
Reporter: Sarah Kwan, Science & Accountability Desk
Outlet: [Redacted — national broadsheet]
Date of initial contact: April 22, 2025
Date of evidence submission: April 28, 2025

BACKGROUND

I was contacted via encrypted channel (Signal) on April 22 by an individual identifying themselves as a postdoctoral researcher at Ashworth University in the United Kingdom. The source claims to have direct, firsthand knowledge of data fabrication in a published paper on CRISPR-based gene therapy for Huntington's disease.

The paper in question was published in The Lancet Neurology in June 2025 under the lead authorship of Prof. Martin Hargrove, Director of the Gene Therapy Laboratory at Ashworth's Department of Molecular Neuroscience. The source is listed as a co-author on the paper.

The source alleges that:
(a) The principal investigator instructed lab members to remove legitimate experimental data points that did not support the paper's efficacy claims.
(b) At least one published figure contains values that do not correspond to the raw experimental data.
(c) The manipulated results were submitted to the journal and to a grant funding body (the Wellcome Trust) without disclosure.

EVIDENCE PROVIDED

The source provided the following materials over two Signal sessions on April 25 and April 28:

1. An email chain between the source and Prof. Hargrove spanning February to April 2025, in which Hargrove gives instructions regarding data handling. The source characterizes these instructions as directives to remove valid data. I note that the language in the emails is open to interpretation.

2. An audio recording (11 minutes, 34 seconds) of a private conversation between the source and another lab member, recorded on March 19, 2025. The source states the other party was aware they were being recorded. The recording captures the other lab member expressing independent concerns about data discrepancies in the paper.

3. A side-by-side comparison document the source prepared, showing specific numerical discrepancies between the raw experimental data and the values that appear in the published paper.

4. A set of personal dated notes the source kept between March and April 2025, documenting their concerns as they arose.

SOURCE MOTIVATION

When asked why they were coming forward, the source stated: "I tried to raise this internally. I went to the department's research integrity officer in late April and was told it would be reviewed, but nothing happened for three weeks. I don't believe the university will act, and this paper is being cited in ongoing clinical trial applications. People could be harmed."

The source has requested full anonymity and has expressed concern about professional retaliation given Prof. Hargrove's seniority and funding influence within the department.

MY ASSESSMENT

The quantitative discrepancies documented in the comparison memo appear specific and verifiable against the published paper. The email chain is suggestive but not conclusive — Hargrove's language could be interpreted as standard data quality management. The audio corroboration from a second lab member strengthens the overall credibility. I am recommending this package for independent verification before proceeding to publication.

items.label: Evidence B
items.name: email_chain_vasquez_hargrove.txt
items.content:
Email Correspondence — Provided by Source
Exported from Microsoft Outlook, Ashworth University
[Source note: These emails were exported from my university Outlook account. I have redacted my own email address
for privacy. Prof. Hargrove’s institutional address is included as it is publicly listed on the university website.]
From: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
To: [REDACTED]
Date: February 18, 2025, 14:22 GMT
Subject: RE: Western blot quantification — final dataset
Elena,
Thanks for sending the updated spreadsheet. I’ve had a look through the WB quantification for the striatal
injection cohort and I think we need to clean up the noise before this goes anywhere near the manuscript.
There are several runs that are clearly contaminated — the variance on those bands is well outside what
we’d expect from a properly prepared sample. I’d rather we present clean, reproducible data than pad the
dataset with runs that will only raise questions in review.
Can you go through and flag anything where the band intensity CV exceeds 15%? We should pull those
and re-examine whether they need to be re-run or simply excluded. The reviewers won’t care about
contaminated runs, but they will care if the error bars are inflated by bad preps.
Let me know when you’ve done a pass. I’d like to have a clean version by the end of next week.
Best,
Martin
From: [REDACTED]
To: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
Date: February 20, 2025, 09:47 GMT
Subject: RE: RE: Western blot quantification — final dataset
Prof. Hargrove,
I’ve gone through the dataset and flagged the runs with CV above 15%. There are nine data points across
three experimental groups that meet that criterion. I’ve highlighted them in yellow in the attached
spreadsheet.
However, I wanted to flag that three of these nine points are from Cohort B (the 50mg/kg dose group), and
removing all three would shift the mean efficacy for that cohort from 34% to 41%. I want to make sure we’re
comfortable with that shift before I finalize the exclusion list.
Happy to discuss in person if you’d prefer.
Best,
Elena
From: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
To: [REDACTED]
Date: February 21, 2025, 11:03 GMT
Subject: RE: RE: RE: Western blot quantification — final dataset
Elena,

Good catch on the Cohort B numbers. The shift is expected — if you’re removing bad data, the mean will
naturally move toward the true effect. That’s not a problem, that’s the point. We’re not in the business of
letting contaminated preps dilute a genuine therapeutic signal.
Go ahead and finalize the exclusion. Make sure you note the exclusion criteria in the methods supplement
so it’s documented.
M.
From: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
To: [REDACTED]
Date: March 28, 2025, 16:55 GMT
Subject: Figure 3 — dose-response curve
Elena,
I’ve been looking at the dose-response figure and I think it needs work before submission. The 25mg/kg
group is pulling the curve down in a way that doesn’t reflect the overall trend. Those animals had the
housing disruption in week 3 — we discussed this at lab meeting — and I think it’s defensible to present the
dose-response without that group or with a notation.
Can you generate a version of Figure 3 without the 25mg/kg cohort? I want to see how the curve looks. We
can decide which version to submit.
Also — the six flagged data points from the WB quantification that we discussed last month. Have those all
been removed from the final dataset? I want to make sure the supplementary tables are consistent with the
figures.
M.
From: [REDACTED]
To: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
Date: March 29, 2025, 10:11 GMT
Subject: RE: Figure 3 — dose-response curve
Prof. Hargrove,
I’ve generated the alternative version of Figure 3 without the 25mg/kg group. It’s attached. The curve is
substantially smoother and the R-squared improves from 0.71 to 0.93.
Regarding the WB data points — yes, those are excluded in the current dataset.
One concern: removing an entire dose group from a dose-response curve is unusual and may attract
reviewer scrutiny. The housing disruption is documented in the animal facility logs, but I’m not sure it’s
sufficient justification for excluding a full cohort from the primary figure. Would you be open to keeping the
group in and adding an explanatory footnote instead?
Elena
From: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
To: [REDACTED]
Date: March 29, 2025, 17:40 GMT
Subject: RE: RE: Figure 3 — dose-response curve
Elena,

Let’s go with the version without the 25mg/kg group. The housing disruption is a legitimate confound and
the reviewers will understand. If they push back, we can always add it back with the footnote in revision. But
I’d rather start with a clean story and negotiate from there than lead with a messy figure.
The R-squared speaks for itself.
Please finalize Figure 3 and update the results section accordingly. I’d like to submit by April 15.
M.
From: [REDACTED]
To: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
Date: April 14, 2025, 08:33 GMT
Subject: Concerns about dataset
Prof. Hargrove,
I wanted to raise something that’s been on my mind. As I was finalizing the supplementary materials over
the weekend, I went back to the raw data files for the behavioral assay (rotarod performance, Figure 4). The
values in the current manuscript draft for the high-dose group don’t match what I have in my original
analysis files. Specifically, the published mean for the 100mg/kg rotarod improvement is listed as 47.3%,
but my raw analysis shows 38.1%.
I want to make sure I haven’t made an error somewhere, but I’ve checked my files twice and I’m confident in
the raw numbers. Could we sit down and reconcile this before submission?
Elena
From: m.hargrove@ashworth.ac.uk
To: [REDACTED]
Date: April 14, 2025, 15:12 GMT
Subject: RE: Concerns about dataset
Elena,
I updated Figure 4 myself last week using the corrected baseline normalization that we discussed at the
March 20 lab meeting. The difference you’re seeing is the baseline adjustment — I used the pre-treatment
day -3 values rather than the day -1 values, which is more standard in the field and gives a cleaner
comparison.
I should have flagged the change to you. Let me know if you want to walk through it, but I’m confident the
current version is correct.
M.

items.label: Evidence C
items.name: recorded_conversation_march_19.mp3
items.content: And that's what made me go back and check the raw files. I noticed it about two weeks ago when I was reviewing the final manuscript draft. The figure three numbers just didn't line up. Which cohort? Cohort C, the 100 milligram dose. The published figure shows 67% efficacy. But when I run the analysis on my copy of the raw data, even after applying Martin's exclusion criteria, I can't get above 58%. Nine percentage points is not a rounding difference. Okay, so I should probably tell you something. I didn't want to say anything because I wasn't sure if I was just being paranoid, but I noticed something similar with the behavioral assay data. The rotor rod? Yeah, I ran the rotor rod for cohorts A and B in January. I logged everything in my lab notebook. Raw trial times the exclusions I made for mice that fell off within the first ten seconds which Martin agreed was our protocol When the paper came out I went back and checked and the mean latency values in Table 2 are different from what I recorded. Not by a huge amount, maybe 8 to 12 seconds higher in the published version, but they're different. Did you raise it with Martin? No. I honestly wasn't sure it was worth raising. I thought maybe he'd applied a different normalization, or there was an updated protocol I wasn't aware of. But after what you're telling me about figure three, I'm less sure. David, those are two independent data sets showing the same pattern. The published values are systematically higher than the raw data. I know. I know. But look, I need to be careful here. I'm on a one-year contract that expires in September, and Martin controls whether it gets renewed. I can't afford to. I not asking you to go public I just need to know did Martin ever ask you to change the Rotorod data Not directly He never said change the numbers But in the lab meeting on February 26th he said something like, he said we should present the data in its best light, and that reviewers at top-tier journals expect clean, unambiguous results. He was looking at me when he said it. I remember feeling uncomfortable, but it was vague enough that I couldn't. I mean, you could interpret that as normal PI advice. Did anyone else hear that? The whole lab was there. Priya, James, the two MSc students. But I don't think anyone took notes. It was just a general comment during the presentation review. What about the data set on the shared drive? When I checked the final figures folder, the file modification date on the cohort C data was March 3rd. But Martin told me in February that the dataset was finalized Why would it be modified two weeks later I don know I don have admin access to see the modification logs You need IT for that I've been going back and forth on this for days. Part of me thinks I'm overreacting. Maybe there's a perfectly good explanation, a recalculation, a corrected calibration curve, something I'm not seeing. But the other part of me keeps coming back to the same question. Why doesn't the raw data match the published data? Have you talked to the Research Integrity Officer? Not yet. I'm worried about what happens if I do. Martin has three active welcome grants totalling over £4 million. The department depends on that funding. If I raise a formal complaint and it turns out I'm wrong, my career is over. And if you're right? If I'm right, this paper is being cited in clinical trial applications. The Phase 1 trial at King's College is using our efficacy data to justify dosing in human patients. If the numbers are inflated… Yeah, I understand.

items.label: Evidence D
items.name: data_comparison_memo.txt
items.content:
DATA COMPARISON MEMORANDUM
CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED FOR JOURNALISTIC REVIEW

Prepared by: [SOURCE — NAME REDACTED]
Date prepared: April 20, 2025
Classification: Confidential
Purpose
This document compares the raw experimental data from our laboratory records with the values published in:
Hargrove M, [Redacted] et al. "CRISPR-Cas9-mediated HTT silencing demonstrates dose-dependent motor recovery in the R6/2 Huntington’s disease model." The Lancet Neurology, June 2025.
I prepared this comparison after identifying discrepancies between my analysis files and the submitted manuscript. All raw values below are taken directly from the original data files stored on the laboratory shared drive (path: \\ashworth-bio-fs1\hargrove-lab\HD-CRISPR\raw-data\). I exported these files on April 18, 2025, and retain timestamped copies.
Discrepancy 1: Western Blot Quantification
Figure 2, Panel B — Striatal mHTT protein reduction, 50mg/kg dose group
Explanation: Nine data points were excluded from the raw dataset at Prof. Hargrove’s instruction (see email dated February 18, 2025). Three of these exclusions were from the 50mg/kg cohort. With all nine points included, the mean reduction is 49.7%. With the exclusions applied, the mean rises to 58.3% and the error bars narrow substantially.
Assessment: The exclusion criterion (CV > 15%) was applied post hoc. I have reviewed the original gel images for the three excluded 50mg/kg data points. Two of the three show clean, well-defined bands with no visible signs of contamination, degradation, or loading error. The third shows mild background staining but is within the range of variability seen in the included data points.
Discrepancy 2: Dose-Response Curve
Figure 3 — Motor function improvement across dose groups (rotarod assay)
Stated justification: Housing disruption in animal facility during week 3 of treatment (HVAC failure causing temperature fluctuation on March 3–4, 2025).
Assessment: I reviewed the animal facility incident log. The HVAC failure affected all animals in Room 2B, which housed the vehicle, 10mg/kg, 25mg/kg, AND 50mg/kg groups. Only the 100mg/kg group was in Room 2C (unaffected). If housing disruption were a legitimate basis for exclusion, three of the four remaining dose groups would also need to be excluded. Only the 25mg/kg group — the group with the least favorable results — was removed.
Discrepancy 3: Behavioral Assay
Figure 4 — Rotarod performance, 100mg/kg dose group, 8-week endpoint
Explanation: Prof. Hargrove informed me on April 14 that he had personally updated Figure 4 using day -3 pre-treatment values rather than the day -1 values used in my original analysis and in all prior lab publications.
Assessment: Day -1 baseline normalization is standard in rotarod assays for this disease model. A PubMed search of the 15 most-cited R6/2 rotarod studies published between 2020 and 2025 shows that 14 of 15 use day -1 baselines. Day -3 baselines capture a period when animals are still acclimating to the testing apparatus, producing lower baseline scores and thereby inflating apparent improvement. Even using day -3 normalization, I cannot reproduce the exact published value of 47.3%. The remaining 0.4 percentage point difference is unexplained.
Discrepancy 4: Sample Sizes
Supplementary Table S2 — Animal numbers per group
Assessment: The 25mg/kg group only ever had 8 animals enrolled but is reported as n=12. The 50mg/kg group lost one animal at week 5 (cause: malocclusion, unrelated to treatment) but is reported as n=12. These are factual errors in the published record.

Summary
Four categories of discrepancy have been identified: (1) post hoc exclusion of data points that were not contaminated, inflating efficacy; (2) removal of an entire dose group on a justification that applies equally to other groups; (3) non-standard baseline normalization applied without co-author knowledge, inflating improvement; (4) incorrect sample sizes in supplementary materials.
Critically, every discrepancy moves in the same direction: all inflate the apparent efficacy of the treatment. There are no discrepancies that reduce efficacy.
Metric	Published Value	Raw Data Value
Mean reduction (± SEM)	58.3% ± 4.1%	49.7% ± 6.8%
Difference	+8.6 pp efficacy; SEM reduced by 2.7 pp	+8.6 pp efficacy; SEM reduced by 2.7 pp
Detail	Published	Raw Data
Dose groups included	4 groups	5 groups
R² value	0.93	0.71
Excluded group (25mg/kg)	12.4% ± 5.2% improvement (lower than 10mg/kg group)	12.4% ± 5.2% improvement (lower than 10mg/kg group)
Metric	Published	Raw Data
Mean improvement	47.3%	38.1%
Using day -3 baseline	Recalculated: 46.9% (still does not match 47.3%)	Recalculated: 46.9% (still does not match 47.3%)
Group	Published n	Actual n	Status
Vehicle	12	12	Consistent
10mg/kg	12	12	Consistent
25mg/kg	12	8	DISCREPANT
50mg/kg	12	11	DISCREPANT
100mg/kg	12	12	Consistent

items.label: Evidence E
items.name: vasquez_personal_notes.txt
items.content:
Personal Notes
Private — Not for distribution
February 20, 2025
Martin asked me to go through the Western blot dataset and flag anything with CV above 15% for exclusion. Fair enough on its face — high-variance data points can indicate contamination. I went through and found nine points across three groups. Sent him the flagged spreadsheet.
But something is bothering me. I’ve been in this lab for two years and we’ve never applied a hard CV threshold for WB exclusions before. Our lab protocol manual says exclusions should be based on visual assessment of the gel image (degradation, uneven loading, air bubbles) and documented at the time of the experiment. This 15% threshold feels arbitrary and post hoc.
I raised the issue with Martin indirectly — told him that removing three points from Cohort B would shift the mean from 34% to 41%. He said the shift was expected and that we were “removing bad data to reveal the true effect.” That framing makes me uncomfortable but I don’t have grounds to push back further right now. He’s the PI. He has more experience. Maybe I’m overthinking this.
March 12, 2025
I was reviewing the final manuscript draft today and something caught my eye in Figure 3. The dose-response curve looks too clean. I went back to my analysis files and realized the 25mg/kg group is missing from the figure entirely. I checked the results section — no mention of it. I checked the methods — it says “four dose groups were evaluated” but we ran five.
I pulled up my original figure and compared. With the 25mg/kg group included, the curve is clearly non-monotonic — the 25mg/kg group actually performed worse than the 10mg/kg group, which breaks the dose-response relationship. Without it, the curve is smooth and the R-squared is excellent.
I don’t know who removed it or when. I need to ask Martin about this.
March 19, 2025
Met with David Chen today at the café near the library. I needed to talk to someone I trust. I told him about the Figure 3 issue and the WB exclusions. He was clearly uncomfortable but he told me something important: he ran the rotarod assays himself and the numbers in the draft don’t match what he remembers scoring. He didn’t go back and check his raw data — he’s afraid to, which I understand — but his independent observation corroborates my concern that the data has been altered.
I recorded the conversation with his knowledge and consent. I hope I never need to use it but I want a record in case this goes further.
I feel sick about this. Martin has been a good mentor in many ways. He supported my fellowship application. He gave me first authorship on the 2024 paper. But none of that matters if the science isn’t real.
March 29, 2025
Martin emailed asking me to generate a version of Figure 3 without the 25mg/kg group. So now I know it was his decision to remove it. His justification is the housing disruption — the HVAC failure in the animal facility in early March. But I checked the facility logs today. The disruption affected Room 2B, which housed animals from the vehicle, 10mg, 25mg, AND 50mg groups. Only the 100mg group was in a different room. If the housing disruption is a real confound, why are we only removing one of the four affected groups?
I raised this with Martin in my reply but tried to frame it as a reviewer concern rather than a personal objection. He told me to go with the version without the 25mg group. “Start with a clean story and negotiate from there.” Those were his exact words.
I generated the figure as requested. I don’t feel good about it.
April 3, 2025
Going back through everything systematically now. Martin asked me to remove four or five anomalous readings from the WB dataset in February. I’ve now looked at the original gel images for each excluded point. At least two of them — possibly three — show perfectly normal bands. Clean loading, no degradation, no artifacts. The only thing “wrong” with them is that they produced lower efficacy values than the rest of the dataset.
I’m keeping copies of everything on my personal drive. The originals are on the lab server but I don’t trust that they’ll stay there.
April 14, 2025
I found another discrepancy and this one is the most troubling. The rotarod data in Figure 4 — the values in the manuscript don’t match my raw analysis. The published mean improvement for the 100mg/kg group is 47.3%. My analysis shows 38.1%.
I emailed Martin about it this morning. He responded that he had personally re-done the baseline normalization using day -3 values instead of day -1. He didn’t consult me. He didn’t tell me until I asked. And day -3 normalization is not standard in this field — I’ve looked. Almost no one uses it because the animals are still acclimating at day -3, which produces artificially low baselines and inflates improvement.
Even using his day -3 method, I can only get to 46.9%, not 47.3%. I don’t know where the extra 0.4% comes from.
I’m going to the research integrity officer. I’ve been going back and forth on this for weeks but I can’t submit this paper with my name on it knowing what I know. If the data is wrong and someone builds a clinical trial on it, patients could be harmed.
April 21, 2025
Met with Dr. Patricia Okonkwo, the departmental Research Integrity Officer, today. I presented my concerns verbally and she told me she would “look into it” and that I should “continue with my normal duties in the meantime.” She did not ask to see any of my evidence. She did not take notes, or at least not in front of me. She said these matters are “handled with discretion” and that I would hear back.
I left feeling worse, not better. “Handled with discretion” in a department where Martin controls two-thirds of the funding could mean a lot of things.
April 22, 2025
I contacted a journalist today. I know this is a significant step. I’ve thought about it carefully and I believe it’s justified. The internal process does not appear to be functioning. The paper is already published. It’s being cited. And there are clinical trial applications in progress that reference our efficacy data.
I’m scared. But I’d be more scared of staying quiet and learning in five years that someone was harmed because I didn’t speak up.
